problems.title: Road Gradient
problems.scenario: A road elevation is modelled by the function h(x) = 3x² + 2x + 5, where h is the height in metres and x is the horizontal distance in km. Find the gradient (slope) of the road at x = 2 km.
problems.question: Find h'(x) and evaluate it at x = 2.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Differentiate h(x) = 3x² + 2x + 5
h'(x) = 6x + 2

Step 2: Evaluate at x = 2
h'(2) = 6(2) + 2 = 12 + 2 = 14

Conclusion: The road gradient at x = 2 km is 14 metres per km.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting to differentiate the constant term correctly — the derivative of a constant is 0, not the constant itself.

problems.title: Water Tank Volume
problems.scenario: Water flows into a tank at a rate given by Q(t) = 4t + 3 litres per minute. Find the total volume of water that flows into the tank in the first 5 minutes, given the tank starts empty.
problems.question: Evaluate the definite integral of Q(t) from t = 0 to t = 5.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Set up the integral
V = ∫₀⁵ (4t + 3) dt

Step 2: Integrate
V = [2t² + 3t]₀⁵

Step 3: Evaluate
V = (2(25) + 15) - (0) = 50 + 15 = 65

Conclusion: 65 litres of water flows into the tank in the first 5 minutes.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting to subtract the lower limit evaluation — always evaluate at both bounds.

problems.title: Beam Deflection
problems.scenario: A simply supported beam of length 6m carries a uniformly distributed load. The bending moment is given by M(x) = 36x - 6x². The deflection y(x) is found by integrating M(x) twice (assuming EI = 1). The boundary conditions are y(0) = 0 and y(6) = 0.
problems.question: Find the deflection equation y(x) by integrating twice and applying boundary conditions.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: First integration (slope)
y'(x) = ∫(36x - 6x²)dx = 18x² - 2x³ + C₁

Step 2: Second integration (deflection)
y(x) = ∫(18x² - 2x³ + C₁)dx = 6x³ - (x⁴/2) + C₁x + C₂

Step 3: Apply y(0) = 0
6(0) - 0 + 0 + C₂ = 0 → C₂ = 0

Step 4: Apply y(6) = 0
6(216) - (1296/2) + 6C₁ = 0
1296 - 648 + 6C₁ = 0
6C₁ = -648 → C₁ = -108

Final Answer: y(x) = 6x³ - x⁴/2 - 108x
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting constants of integration at each step — every integration introduces a new constant.

problems.title: Concrete Pour Rate
problems.scenario: The rate of concrete being poured into a foundation is modelled by R(t) = 12t² - 3t + 8 m³/hour. Find the total volume poured between t = 1 hour and t = 4 hours.
problems.question: Evaluate the definite integral of R(t) from t = 1 to t = 4.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Set up the integral
V = ∫₁⁴ (12t² - 3t + 8) dt

Step 2: Integrate
V = [4t³ - (3t²/2) + 8t]₁⁴

Step 3: Evaluate at t = 4
4(64) - (3(16)/2) + 8(4) = 256 - 24 + 32 = 264

Step 4: Evaluate at t = 1
4(1) - (3/2) + 8 = 4 - 1.5 + 8 = 10.5

Step 5: Subtract
V = 264 - 10.5 = 253.5 m³
problems.common_mistake: Evaluating only at the upper limit and forgetting to subtract the lower limit evaluation.

problems.title: Maximum Load on a Cantilever Beam
problems.scenario: A cantilever beam has a deflection function y(x) = (w/24EI)(x⁴ - 4Lx³ + 6L²x²), where w is the load per unit length, L is the beam length, E is Young's modulus, and I is the second moment of area. Find the location of maximum deflection.
problems.question: Differentiate y(x) and set y'(x) = 0 to find the location of maximum deflection.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Differentiate y(x)
y'(x) = (w/24EI)(4x³ - 12Lx² + 12L²x)

Step 2: Factor out common terms
y'(x) = (w/24EI)(4x)(x² - 3Lx + 3L²)

Step 3: Set y'(x) = 0
Either x = 0 (fixed end, trivial solution)
Or x² - 3Lx + 3L² = 0

Step 4: Apply quadratic formula
x = (3L ± √(9L² - 12L²))/2
The discriminant is negative — no real roots.

Conclusion: Maximum deflection occurs at the free end x = L.
problems.answer: Maximum deflection at x = L (free end)
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting to check whether critical points are maxima or minima using the second derivative test.

problems.title: Fluid Flow Through a Pipe
problems.scenario: The velocity profile of fluid flowing through a circular pipe of radius R is given by v(r) = V_max(1 - r²/R²), where r is the radial distance from the centre. Find the average velocity across the pipe cross-section.
problems.question: Use integration to find the average velocity: v_avg = (1/A)∫v dA, where dA = 2πr dr and A = πR².
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Set up the integral
v_avg = (1/πR²) ∫₀ᴿ V_max(1 - r²/R²)(2πr) dr

Step 2: Simplify
v_avg = (2V_max/R²) ∫₀ᴿ (r - r³/R²) dr

Step 3: Integrate
v_avg = (2V_max/R²) [r²/2 - r⁴/(4R²)]₀ᴿ

Step 4: Evaluate
v_avg = (2V_max/R²)(R²/2 - R²/4)
v_avg = (2V_max/R²)(R²/4)
v_avg = V_max/2
problems.common_mistake: Using dA = dr instead of the correct annular area element dA = 2πr dr for circular cross-sections.

problems.title: Roof Pitch Angle
problems.scenario: A roof rises 3.5 metres over a horizontal run of 8 metres. A civil engineer needs to find the pitch angle to specify the correct roofing materials.
problems.question: Find the pitch angle θ of the roof.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Identify the relationship
tan(θ) = opposite/adjacent = rise/run = 3.5/8

Step 2: Solve for θ
θ = arctan(3.5/8) = arctan(0.4375)

Step 3: Calculate
θ ≈ 23.6°

Conclusion: The roof pitch angle is approximately 23.6°.
problems.common_mistake: Using sin or cos instead of tan when both the opposite and adjacent sides are known.

problems.title: Bridge Cable Length
problems.scenario: A suspension bridge cable is anchored at a point 20 metres above the deck and makes an angle of 35° with the horizontal deck surface. Find the length of the cable from anchor to deck attachment point.
problems.question: Find the cable length L.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Identify the triangle
The vertical height = 20m, angle with horizontal = 35°
sin(35°) = opposite/hypotenuse = 20/L

Step 2: Solve for L
L = 20/sin(35°)

Step 3: Calculate
L = 20/0.5736 ≈ 34.9 metres

Conclusion: The cable length is approximately 34.9 metres.
problems.common_mistake: Confusing which side is opposite and which is adjacent relative to the given angle.

problems.title: Truss Force Resolution
problems.scenario: A roof truss member carries a force of 50 kN at an angle of 40° to the horizontal. A civil engineer needs to resolve this into horizontal and vertical components to check joint equilibrium.
problems.question: Find the horizontal (Fx) and vertical (Fy) components of the 50 kN force.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Resolve horizontal component
Fx = F × cos(θ) = 50 × cos(40°)
Fx = 50 × 0.766 = 38.3 kN

Step 2: Resolve vertical component
Fy = F × sin(θ) = 50 × sin(40°)
Fy = 50 × 0.643 = 32.1 kN

Step 3: Verify using Pythagoras
√(38.3² + 32.1²) = √(1466.9 + 1030.4) = √2497.3 ≈ 50 kN ✓
problems.common_mistake: Swapping sin and cos — remember cos gives the component along the reference direction (horizontal), sin gives the perpendicular component.

problems.title: Setting Out a Building Corner
problems.scenario: A surveyor needs to set out a rectangular building on site. Starting from point A, they measure 25m due East to point B. The building corner C is 18m from B at an angle of 72° from the line AB. Find the distance AC and the angle at A.
problems.question: Using the cosine rule, find AC. Then use the sine rule to find angle A.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Apply the Cosine Rule
AC² = AB² + BC² - 2(AB)(BC)cos(B)
AC² = 25² + 18² - 2(25)(18)cos(72°)
AC² = 625 + 324 - 900(0.309)
AC² = 949 - 278.1 = 670.9
AC = √670.9 ≈ 25.9m

Step 2: Apply the Sine Rule to find angle A
sin(A)/BC = sin(B)/AC
sin(A) = 18 × sin(72°)/25.9
sin(A) = 18 × 0.951/25.9 = 0.661
Angle A = arcsin(0.661) ≈ 41.3°
problems.common_mistake: Using the sine rule when the cosine rule is needed — the sine rule requires a known angle-opposite side pair first.

problems.title: Wind Load on a Pitched Roof
problems.scenario: A pitched roof makes an angle of 30° with the horizontal. Wind pressure acts perpendicular to the roof surface with a pressure of 1.2 kN/m². The roof panel is 6m wide and 10m long. Find the total horizontal and vertical components of the wind force on the roof.
problems.question: Find Fx (horizontal) and Fy (vertical) components of the total wind force.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Calculate total wind force
Area of roof panel = 6 × 10 = 60 m²
Total force F = 1.2 × 60 = 72 kN (perpendicular to roof)

Step 2: The force is perpendicular to the roof surface
The roof is at 30° to horizontal, so the force is at (90° - 30°) = 60° to horizontal

Step 3: Resolve components
Fx = F × sin(30°) = 72 × 0.5 = 36 kN (horizontal)
Fy = F × cos(30°) = 72 × 0.866 = 62.4 kN (vertical)

Conclusion: Horizontal force = 36 kN, Vertical force = 62.4 kN
problems.common_mistake: Not accounting for the direction of the perpendicular force correctly — the force perpendicular to the roof is NOT the same angle as the roof pitch relative to horizontal.

problems.title: Retaining Wall Soil Pressure
problems.scenario: A retaining wall holds back soil. The resultant soil pressure acts at an angle of 20° below horizontal with a magnitude of 85 kN/m. The wall is 4m high. Find the overturning moment about the base of the wall due to the horizontal component of this force, assuming the force acts at one-third of the wall height from the base.
problems.question: Find the horizontal component of force and the overturning moment about the base.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Find horizontal component
Fx = 85 × cos(20°) = 85 × 0.940 = 79.9 kN/m

Step 2: Find vertical component (for completeness)
Fy = 85 × sin(20°) = 85 × 0.342 = 29.1 kN/m

Step 3: Find point of application
Height = (1/3) × 4 = 1.333m from base

Step 4: Calculate overturning moment
M = Fx × height = 79.9 × 1.333 = 106.5 kNm/m

Conclusion: Overturning moment = 106.5 kNm per metre length of wall.
problems.common_mistake: Using the full wall height instead of one-third height for the point of application of triangular soil pressure distribution.

problems.title: Simultaneous Equations — Material Costs
problems.scenario: A construction company uses two materials, concrete (x tonnes) and steel (y tonnes), for a project. The total weight is 50 tonnes and the total cost is €120,000. Concrete costs €1,500/tonne and steel costs €3,500/tonne.
problems.question: Set up and solve the system of simultaneous equations to find x and y.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Set up equations
x + y = 50  ...(1)
1500x + 3500y = 120000  ...(2)

Step 2: From equation (1)
x = 50 - y

Step 3: Substitute into equation (2)
1500(50 - y) + 3500y = 120000
75000 - 1500y + 3500y = 120000
2000y = 45000
y = 22.5 tonnes (steel)

Step 4: Find x
x = 50 - 22.5 = 27.5 tonnes (concrete)

Verification: 1500(27.5) + 3500(22.5) = 41250 + 78750 = €120,000 ✓
problems.common_mistake: Setting up the cost equation incorrectly by mixing up which coefficient belongs to which material.

problems.title: Matrix Addition — Load Combinations
problems.scenario: A structural engineer analyses two load cases for a three-storey building. Dead loads (kN) per floor are [120, 95, 60] and live loads (kN) per floor are [45, 38, 25]. Find the total load per floor.
problems.question: Add the two load vectors to find the total load on each floor.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Represent as vectors
Dead loads: D = [120, 95, 60]
Live loads: L = [45, 38, 25]

Step 2: Add element by element
Floor 1: 120 + 45 = 165 kN
Floor 2: 95 + 38 = 133 kN
Floor 3: 60 + 25 = 85 kN

Total load vector T = [165, 133, 85] kN

Conclusion: The total loads per floor are 165 kN, 133 kN, and 85 kN respectively.
problems.common_mistake: Adding all elements together into a single sum instead of adding corresponding elements (floor by floor).

problems.title: Truss Analysis Using Matrix Methods
problems.scenario:
A simple pin-jointed truss has two unknown member forces F1 and F2. At a joint in equilibrium, the force balance equations are:
2F1 + F2 = 80
F1 + 3F2 = 90
problems.question: Express the system as a matrix equation [A][F] = [b] and solve using the inverse matrix method.
problems.common_mistake: Incorrectly computing the inverse — remember for a 2×2 matrix, swap the diagonal elements and negate the off-diagonal elements, then divide by the determinant.

problems.title: Coordinate Transformation — Site Survey
problems.scenario: A surveyor's measurements are in a local coordinate system. The transformation to the national grid requires a rotation of 25°. A point measured at local coordinates (100m, 50m) needs to be converted to national grid coordinates.
problems.question: Apply the 2D rotation matrix to transform the coordinates.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Write the rotation matrix for θ = 25°
R = [cos25°  -sin25°]
    [sin25°   cos25°]
R = [0.906   -0.423]
    [0.423    0.906]

Step 2: Multiply R × [100, 50]ᵀ
X_national = 0.906(100) + (-0.423)(50) = 90.6 - 21.15 = 69.45m
Y_national = 0.423(100) + 0.906(50) = 42.3 + 45.3 = 87.6m

Conclusion: National grid coordinates are approximately (69.45m, 87.6m).
problems.common_mistake: Using the wrong sign convention — a counter-clockwise rotation has -sin in the top right and +sin in the bottom left of the rotation matrix.

problems.title: Stiffness Matrix — Spring Analogy for Structural Frame
problems.scenario:
A two-storey structural frame can be modelled using a stiffness matrix approach. The stiffness matrix K and displacement vector u are related to the force vector F by K·u = F.
K = [[6, -2], [-2, 4]] (kN/mm)
F = [10, 6] kN
Find the displacements u1 and u2.
problems.question: Solve K·u = F using the inverse matrix method.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Find determinant of K
det(K) = (6×4) - (-2×-2) = 24 - 4 = 20

Step 2: Find K⁻¹
K⁻¹ = (1/20)[ 4   2]
             [ 2   6]

Step 3: Solve u = K⁻¹ · F
u1 = (1/20)(4×10 + 2×6) = (40 + 12)/20 = 52/20 = 2.6 mm
u2 = (1/20)(2×10 + 6×6) = (20 + 36)/20 = 56/20 = 2.8 mm

Conclusion: Floor 1 displaces 2.6mm, Floor 2 displaces 2.8mm under the applied loads.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting that matrix multiplication is not commutative — u = K⁻¹·F, not F·K⁻¹.

problems.title: Eigenvalue Analysis — Natural Frequency of a Structure
problems.scenario: A two-degree-of-freedom structural system has a stiffness matrix K = [[5, -2], [-2, 3]] and a mass matrix M = [[2, 0], [0, 1]] (simplified units). The natural frequencies are found from the eigenvalue problem det(K - ω²M) = 0.
problems.question: Find the natural frequencies ω by solving the characteristic equation.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting to take the square root at the end — λ = ω², so you must find √λ to get the actual frequency.

problems.title: Drainage Rate
problems.scenario: Water drains from a construction site sump. The volume V (m³) of water decreases at a rate proportional to the current volume, described by dV/dt = -0.3V. Initially there are 200 m³ of water.
problems.question: Find V(t) and determine how much water remains after 4 hours.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Identify the equation
dV/dt = -0.3V → exponential decay

Step 2: General solution
V(t) = V₀ × e^(-0.3t)

Step 3: Apply initial condition V(0) = 200
V(t) = 200e^(-0.3t)

Step 4: Evaluate at t = 4
V(4) = 200e^(-1.2) = 200 × 0.3012 = 60.2 m³

Conclusion: Approximately 60.2 m³ remains after 4 hours.
problems.common_mistake: Using V(t) = V₀ × e^(+kt) (positive exponent) for a decay problem — the decay constant must be negative.

problems.title: Settlement Rate
problems.scenario: The settlement s (mm) of a foundation over time follows ds/dt = 5e^(-0.2t) mm/day. Find the total settlement after 10 days, assuming s(0) = 0.
problems.question: Integrate ds/dt to find s(t) and evaluate at t = 10 days.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting to divide by the coefficient of t in the exponent when integrating exponentials — ∫e^(at)dt = e^(at)/a, not just e^(at).

problems.title: Cooling of Freshly Poured Concrete
problems.scenario: Freshly poured concrete generates heat during curing. Its temperature T(°C) follows Newton's Law of Cooling: dT/dt = -k(T - T_amb), where T_amb = 15°C and k = 0.05 hr⁻¹. The initial concrete temperature is 75°C.
problems.question: Solve the differential equation and find the temperature after 10 hours.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Rewrite the equation
dT/dt = -0.05(T - 15)

Step 2: Substitute u = T - 15, so du/dt = dT/dt
du/dt = -0.05u

Step 3: Solve
u(t) = u₀ × e^(-0.05t)
u₀ = T(0) - 15 = 75 - 15 = 60
u(t) = 60e^(-0.05t)

Step 4: Back-substitute
T(t) = 15 + 60e^(-0.05t)

Step 5: Evaluate at t = 10
T(10) = 15 + 60e^(-0.5) = 15 + 60(0.6065) = 15 + 36.4 = 51.4°C
problems.common_mistake: Solving as if T_amb = 0, giving T(t) = 75e^(-0.05t) — always shift by subtracting the ambient temperature before solving.

problems.title: Groundwater Seepage
problems.scenario: Groundwater seeps into an excavation at a rate given by the differential equation dQ/dt + 0.4Q = 12, where Q is the flow rate in m³/hr. Initially Q(0) = 0.
problems.question: Solve this first-order linear ODE using the integrating factor method.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Identify p(t) = 0.4, q(t) = 12

Step 2: Find integrating factor
μ(t) = e^(∫0.4 dt) = e^(0.4t)

Step 3: Multiply both sides by μ
d/dt[e^(0.4t)Q] = 12e^(0.4t)

Step 4: Integrate both sides
e^(0.4t)Q = 12e^(0.4t)/0.4 + C = 30e^(0.4t) + C

Step 5: Solve for Q
Q(t) = 30 + Ce^(-0.4t)

Step 6: Apply Q(0) = 0
0 = 30 + C → C = -30
Q(t) = 30(1 - e^(-0.4t))

Conclusion: The flow rate approaches a steady state of 30 m³/hr.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting to multiply the right-hand side by the integrating factor as well — both sides must be multiplied.

problems.title: Vibration of a Bridge Deck
problems.scenario: A bridge deck subject to wind loading can be modelled as a damped harmonic oscillator: d²y/dt² + 2(dy/dt) + 5y = 0, where y is the vertical displacement in mm. Initial conditions: y(0) = 10mm, y'(0) = 0.
problems.question: Solve this second-order ODE and classify the damping behaviour.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Write the characteristic equation
r² + 2r + 5 = 0

Step 2: Solve using quadratic formula
r = (-2 ± √(4 - 20))/2 = (-2 ± √(-16))/2 = -1 ± 2i

Step 3: Identify damping type
Complex roots with negative real part → Underdamped oscillation

Step 4: Write general solution
y(t) = e^(-t)(A cos(2t) + B sin(2t))

Step 5: Apply y(0) = 10
10 = e^0(A cos0 + B sin0) = A → A = 10

Step 6: Apply y'(0) = 0
y'(t) = -e^(-t)(A cos2t + B sin2t) + e^(-t)(-2A sin2t + 2B cos2t)
y'(0) = -A + 2B = 0 → B = A/2 = 5

Final: y(t) = e^(-t)(10cos2t + 5sin2t)
problems.common_mistake: Confusing underdamped (complex roots), overdamped (two real negative roots), and critically damped (repeated real roots) — always check the discriminant first.

problems.title: Consolidation of Clay Soil
problems.scenario: The consolidation of a saturated clay layer is modelled by: du/dt = Cv(d²u/dx²), where u is excess pore water pressure and Cv is the coefficient of consolidation. For a simplified 1D case, after non-dimensionalisation, the degree of consolidation U(T) ≈ 1 - (8/π²)e^(-π²T/4) for early time. Find the time factor T when U = 90%.
problems.question: Solve for T when U(T) = 0.90.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Set up the equation
0.90 = 1 - (8/π²)e^(-π²T/4)

Step 2: Rearrange
(8/π²)e^(-π²T/4) = 0.10
e^(-π²T/4) = 0.10 × π²/8 = 0.10 × 1.2337 = 0.12337

Step 3: Take natural log
-π²T/4 = ln(0.12337) = -2.091

Step 4: Solve for T
T = (4 × 2.091)/π² = 8.364/9.870 = 0.848

Conclusion: T ≈ 0.848 (Time factor for 90% consolidation)
problems.common_mistake: Not rearranging properly before taking logarithms — isolate the exponential term completely before applying ln to both sides.

problems.title: Concrete Strength — Mean and Standard Deviation
problems.scenario: A civil engineer tests 6 concrete cube samples. The compressive strengths (MPa) are: 32, 35, 31, 36, 33, 34. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the compressive strength.
problems.question: Find the mean (x̄) and sample standard deviation (s).
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Calculate mean
x̄ = (32+35+31+36+33+34)/6 = 201/6 = 33.5 MPa

Step 2: Calculate deviations from mean
(32-33.5)² = 2.25
(35-33.5)² = 2.25
(31-33.5)² = 6.25
(36-33.5)² = 6.25
(33-33.5)² = 0.25
(34-33.5)² = 0.25

Step 3: Sum of squared deviations = 17.5

Step 4: Sample standard deviation
s = √(17.5/(6-1)) = √(3.5) = 1.87 MPa

Conclusion: Mean strength = 33.5 MPa, Standard deviation = 1.87 MPa
problems.common_mistake: Dividing by n instead of (n-1) for sample standard deviation — use (n-1) for samples, n only for the entire population.

problems.title: Probability of Defective Bricks
problems.scenario: A brick manufacturer produces bricks where 4% are defective. A site engineer randomly selects 3 bricks. What is the probability that exactly one brick is defective?
problems.question: Use the binomial probability formula P(X=k) = C(n,k) × p^k × (1-p)^(n-k).
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Identify parameters
n = 3 (number of trials)
k = 1 (number of defectives wanted)
p = 0.04 (probability of defective)

Step 2: Calculate C(3,1)
C(3,1) = 3!/(1! × 2!) = 3

Step 3: Apply formula
P(X=1) = 3 × (0.04)¹ × (0.96)²
P(X=1) = 3 × 0.04 × 0.9216
P(X=1) = 3 × 0.03686 = 0.1106

Conclusion: There is approximately an 11.1% chance that exactly one of the three bricks is defective.
problems.common_mistake: Forgetting the combinatorial coefficient C(n,k) — there are multiple ways to choose which brick is defective, and each must be counted.

problems.title: Confidence Interval — Steel Yield Strength
problems.scenario: A structural engineer tests 25 steel samples and finds a mean yield strength of 420 MPa with a sample standard deviation of 18 MPa. Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean yield strength.
problems.question: Calculate the 95% confidence interval using the formula: x̄ ± z(s/√n), where z = 1.96 for 95% confidence.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Identify values
x̄ = 420 MPa
s = 18 MPa
n = 25
z = 1.96

Step 2: Calculate standard error
SE = s/√n = 18/√25 = 18/5 = 3.6 MPa

Step 3: Calculate margin of error
ME = z × SE = 1.96 × 3.6 = 7.056 MPa

Step 4: Construct interval
Lower: 420 - 7.056 = 412.9 MPa
Upper: 420 + 7.056 = 427.1 MPa

Conclusion: We are 95% confident the true mean yield strength lies between 412.9 MPa and 427.1 MPa.
problems.common_mistake: Using s directly instead of s/√n — the confidence interval uses the standard error (SE), not the raw standard deviation.

problems.title: Linear Regression — Load vs Settlement
problems.scenario:
A geotechnical engineer records the following load-settlement data for a foundation:
Load (kN): 0, 50, 100, 150, 200
Settlement (mm): 0, 2.1, 4.3, 6.0, 8.2
Fit a linear regression model settlement = a + b(load).
problems.question: Calculate the slope b and intercept a using least squares regression formulas.
problems.common_mistake: Using the wrong formula for slope — b = Σ(xi-x̄)(yi-ȳ) / Σ(xi-x̄)², not Σ(xi)(yi) / Σ(xi)².

problems.title: Hypothesis Testing — Concrete Strength Compliance
problems.scenario: A specification requires concrete with a mean compressive strength of at least 40 MPa. A site engineer tests 16 samples and finds x̄ = 38.5 MPa with s = 4 MPa. Test at 5% significance level whether the concrete meets specification (one-tailed test).
problems.question: Perform a one-sample t-test. H₀: μ = 40, H₁: μ < 40.
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Calculate test statistic
t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) = (38.5 - 40)/(4/√16)
t = -1.5/(4/4) = -1.5/1 = -1.5

Step 2: Find critical value
Degrees of freedom = n - 1 = 15
For one-tailed test at 5%, t_critical = -1.753

Step 3: Decision rule
Reject H₀ if t < -1.753
Our t = -1.5 > -1.753

Step 4: Conclusion
Fail to reject H₀. There is insufficient evidence at the 5% level to conclude the concrete fails to meet specification.

Note: This does not prove the concrete is adequate — it means the sample didn't provide enough evidence to reject the specification.
problems.answer: Fail to reject H₀. Insufficient evidence of non-compliance at 5% significance.
problems.common_mistake: Confusing 'fail to reject H₀' with 'accept H₀' — we never prove the null hypothesis, we only fail to find evidence against it.

problems.title: Reliability — Probability of Structural Failure
problems.scenario: The load S on a structural member follows a Normal distribution N(150 kN, 20² kN²). The resistance R follows N(200 kN, 25² kN²). Failure occurs when S > R, i.e., when (S - R) > 0. Find the probability of failure.
problems.question: Model Z = R - S and find P(Z < 0).
problems.worked_solution:
Step 1: Find distribution of Z = R - S
Mean: μ_Z = μ_R - μ_S = 200 - 150 = 50 kN
Variance: σ²_Z = σ²_R + σ²_S = 625 + 400 = 1025 kN²
Std Dev: σ_Z = √1025 = 32.02 kN

Step 2: Standardise
P(Z < 0) = P(Z_std < (0 - 50)/32.02)
P(Z < 0) = P(Z_std < -1.562)

Step 3: Look up standard normal table
P(Z_std < -1.562) ≈ 0.059

Conclusion: The probability of structural failure is approximately 5.9%.
Reliability Index β = 50/32.02 = 1.562
problems.common_mistake: Adding variances incorrectly — when subtracting two independent normal random variables, their variances ADD (not subtract).
